content.text: Multi-Head Attention (MHA) is the core computational primitive of the Transformer architecture, introduced in the 2017 paper "Attention Is All You Need" by Vaswani et al. It allows a model to simultaneously attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions. Rather than computing a single attention function over the full dimensional query, key, and value vectors, MHA linearly projects them into a set of lower-dimensional subspaces, runs scaled dot-product attention in each subspace in parallel, and then concatenates and re-projects the results.

At its core, MHA is a soft, differentiable lookup mechanism. Given a set of query vectors, it returns a weighted sum of value vectors, where the weights are determined by the compatibility between each query and each key. The "multi-head" aspect means this lookup is performed h times simultaneously, each head using its own learned projection matrices. This gives the model h distinct "perspectives" on the same input — one head might track syntactic dependencies, another coreference, another positional proximity — all learned end-to-end from data without explicit supervision.

MHA appears in three distinct roles within the Transformer: as encoder self-attention (every token attends to all other tokens in the input), as decoder self-attention (with causal masking so each position only attends to positions before it), and as encoder-decoder cross-attention (decoder queries attend to encoder key-value pairs). Understanding which variant you are working with is essential for correct implementation.

Before attention mechanisms, sequence models were dominated by recurrent architectures (RNNs, LSTMs, GRUs). These models process tokens sequentially, which imposes two fundamental problems: gradient signals must travel through O(T) steps to connect distant tokens, making long-range dependencies hard to learn; and sequential processing prevents parallelization across the time dimension during training, limiting throughput.

Attention solves the first problem by providing a direct, O(1) connection path between any two positions in a sequence. The "multi-head" extension solves a subtler problem: a single attention head averages information across value vectors, which can wash out fine-grained distinctions. By using h heads with smaller d_k = d_model/h dimensions, the model can jointly attend to multiple types of information at once and combine them through the output projection. This is empirically crucial — GPT, BERT, T5, LLaMA, and virtually every production language model uses multi-head attention as its central mechanism.

For agentic AI systems specifically, MHA matters because it enables long-context reasoning — allowing agents to track goals, tool call results, memory traces, and conversation history spanning tens of thousands of tokens. Modern models like Claude extend this to hundreds of thousands of tokens, all made tractable by efficient attention implementations built on this same mathematical foundation.

Think of multi-head attention as a panel of specialists reviewing a document simultaneously. Each specialist (head) is given a simplified summary of each sentence (the projected queries, keys, values) and independently decides which other sentences are most relevant to each sentence they are currently examining. One specialist might focus on grammatical agreement, another on topic coherence, another on the identity of named entities. Their individual verdicts — weighted summaries of the document — are then concatenated and passed through a chairperson (the output projection W_O) who synthesizes all opinions into a single coherent representation.

A complementary view: think of Q, K, V as a parameterized database lookup. The query Q says "what am I looking for?", the keys K say "what information does each position advertise?", and the values V contain "the actual information to retrieve". The scaled dot-product computes compatibility scores between query and every key, softmax normalizes those into a probability distribution (the attention weights), and the weighted sum over values retrieves a soft mixture of the relevant information. Running h such lookups in parallel — each in a reduced-dimension subspace — lets the model ask h different questions simultaneously.
Any two positions in a sequence are connected by a path of length 1 in the attention graph, compared to O(T) for RNNs. This dramatically simplifies learning long-range dependencies.
All attention scores for all positions are computed in a single batched matrix multiplication (Q @ K.T), enabling near-full GPU utilization during training, unlike sequential RNNs.
Each head learns to attend to different types of information (syntax, coreference, topic, position). This specialization is not hand-coded — it emerges from training on data.
Attention weights are explicit scalars that can be extracted and visualized per head, giving insight into what the model is "looking at". This is imperfect but unique among deep learning primitives.
The Q, K, V inputs can come from different sources (cross-attention) or the same source (self-attention), making MHA a unified primitive for both encoding and decoding.
Every state-of-the-art language, vision, and multimodal model as of 2025 uses MHA or a close variant. The architecture scales predictably with compute and data.

The Q @ K.T operation produces an (T x T) matrix, making naive attention O(T^2) in memory and compute. For a sequence of 32k tokens with d_model=4096 and 32 heads, attention weights alone require ~4GB of float32 memory per layer. Flash Attention and related algorithms mitigate this through kernel fusion but the quadratic scaling remains a fundamental constraint.

MHA is permutation-equivariant by design — it treats the input as an unordered set. Positional information (token order, relative distance) must be injected separately via positional encodings, which adds design complexity.

The four weight matrices (W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O) each have shape d_model x d_model, totaling 4 x d_model^2 parameters per MHA layer. For GPT-3 with d_model=12288, that is roughly 2.4B parameters just for the 96 MHA layers.

Each query attends to all tokens with equal O(T) budget. For tasks where only a few tokens are relevant (sparse attention), compute is wasted. Sparse attention variants exist but complicate implementation.

The power of Transformers comes from MHA plus feed-forward sublayers, layer normalization, and residual connections. MHA alone cannot model the non-linear transformations that give Transformers their expressive power.
The fundamental building block is scaled dot-product attention. Given matrices Q (queries), K (keys), and V (values), it computes:
is the dimensionality of each key (and query) vector. The scaling factor

is essential: without it, when d_k is large, the dot products grow in magnitude, pushing the softmax into regions with near-zero gradients. The scaling counteracts this effect, keeping the softmax in a reasonable operating range regardless of d_k.

has shape (seq_len, seq_len). After softmax, each row sums to 1, giving a probability distribution over positions for each query position. Multiplying by V then produces a weighted sum of value vectors — a soft retrieval.
Multi-head attention runs h instances of scaled dot-product attention in parallel, each with its own learned projections of dimension d_k = d_v = d_model / h:
. In practice, all h heads are computed as a single batched matrix multiply by reshaping the projected Q, K, V tensors from (B, T, d_model) to (B, h, T, d_k), not by running h separate Linear layers.
An additive mask is applied to the raw scores before softmax. For causal (decoder) self-attention, position j is masked when j > i. The mask value is

(implemented as a large negative number like -1e9 or float('-inf')), which drives softmax to output 0 for those positions. For padding masks, all tokens beyond the actual sequence length are similarly masked out to prevent them from influencing attention weights.

The diagram below shows the complete data flow through one Multi-Head Attention layer. The input embeddings are projected into Q, K, and V spaces independently for each head (logically — in practice, a single large linear is split after the multiply). All h heads operate in parallel. Their outputs are concatenated along the last dimension, restoring the original d_model width, and passed through the output projection W^O.

attrs.alt: Multi-Head Attention architecture diagram showing input embeddings, parallel attention heads, concatenation, and output projection

content.text: In a standard Transformer encoder layer, the MHA sublayer is followed by a residual connection and layer normalization:
. This residual path allows gradients to flow without diminishing through the attention computation, and is what makes very deep (96+ layer) Transformers trainable.

The hyperparameters defining an MHA layer are: d_model (the embedding dimension flowing through the network), num_heads (h), and optionally an attention dropout probability. The per-head dimension is derived: d_k = d_model / h. The original paper uses d_model = 512, h = 8, d_k = 64. GPT-3 uses d_model = 12288, h = 96, d_k = 128.
Processing sequential data where global context matters — language modeling, machine translation, summarization, and code generation all benefit from direct token-to-token connections.
Building encoder models (BERT-style) where bidirectional context is needed — the full token sequence should inform each position's representation.
Building decoder models (GPT-style) where autoregressive generation requires causal masking — future tokens must not influence current predictions during inference.
Using cross-attention in encoder-decoder models — the decoder needs to selectively retrieve information from the encoder's representation of the source sequence.

Applying Transformer architectures to non-sequence data by treating patches, nodes, or entities as tokens — vision Transformers (ViT), graph Transformers, and relational reasoning models all rely on MHA.

The sequence length is very large (> 100k tokens) and memory is a hard constraint. Quadratic memory scaling makes naive MHA infeasible. Use sparse attention (Longformer, BigBird), Flash Attention for compute reduction, or linear attention approximations.

Deploying on resource-constrained hardware (microcontrollers, edge devices) where the O(T^2) compute and projection matrix parameters cannot be accommodated. Quantized or distilled variants with fewer heads and smaller d_model may be required.
The task is purely local pattern recognition with no long-range structure — convolutional networks may be more parameter-efficient in such cases.

The canonical implementation follows five steps: project inputs into Q, K, and V; reshape to separate the head dimension; compute scaled dot-product attention in parallel across all heads; concatenate head outputs; apply the final output projection. The following is a complete, self-contained PyTorch implementation:

import math
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class ScaledDotProductAttention(nn.Module):
    """Single scaled dot-product attention head."""

    def __init__(self, dropout: float = 0.0):
        super().__init__()
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(self, Q, K, V, mask=None):
        """
        Args:
            Q: (batch, heads, seq_len, d_k)
            K: (batch, heads, seq_len, d_k)
            V: (batch, heads, seq_len, d_v)
            mask: optional boolean tensor, True means mask out
        Returns:
            output: (batch, heads, seq_len, d_v)
            weights: (batch, heads, seq_len, seq_len)
        """
        d_k = Q.size(-1)
        # Dot-product scores, scaled to prevent vanishing gradients in softmax
        scores = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(d_k)

        if mask is not None:
            scores = scores.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))

        weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
        weights = self.dropout(weights)
        output = torch.matmul(weights, V)
        return output, weights


class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
    """Multi-Head Attention as described in 'Attention Is All You Need'."""

    def __init__(self, d_model: int, num_heads: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % num_heads == 0, "d_model must be divisible by num_heads"

        self.d_model = d_model
        self.h = num_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads  # dimension per head

        # Projection matrices — one Linear handles all heads at once
        self.W_Q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_K = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_V = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_O = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)

        self.attention = ScaledDotProductAttention(dropout)

    def split_heads(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Reshape (batch, seq, d_model) -> (batch, heads, seq, d_k)."""
        B, T, _ = x.shape
        return x.view(B, T, self.h, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)

    def forward(self, Q_in, K_in, V_in, mask=None):
        B = Q_in.size(0)
        # Project and split into heads
        Q = self.split_heads(self.W_Q(Q_in))
        K = self.split_heads(self.W_K(K_in))
        V = self.split_heads(self.W_V(V_in))

        # Attend in parallel across all heads
        attended, weights = self.attention(Q, K, V, mask)

        # Concatenate heads: (batch, heads, seq, d_k) -> (batch, seq, d_model)
        attended = attended.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, -1, self.d_model)

        # Final linear projection
        return self.W_O(attended), weights


# Quick smoke test
if __name__ == "__main__":
    B, T, d_model, h = 2, 10, 512, 8
    mha = MultiHeadAttention(d_model=d_model, num_heads=h, dropout=0.0)
    x = torch.randn(B, T, d_model)
    out, attn_weights = mha(x, x, x)          # self-attention
    print("Output shape:", out.shape)          # (2, 10, 512)
    print("Attn weights:", attn_weights.shape) # (2, 8, 10, 10)

For decoder-only language models, attention must be masked so position i cannot attend to positions j > i. The following shows a production-style implementation with a fused QKV projection, which is faster than three separate Linear layers:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import math

def causal_mask(seq_len: int, device=None) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Upper-triangular mask: position i cannot attend to j > i."""
    return torch.triu(torch.ones(seq_len, seq_len, device=device), diagonal=1).bool()


class CausalMultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
    """Self-attention with causal (autoregressive) masking for decoder stacks."""

    def __init__(self, d_model: int, num_heads: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % num_heads == 0
        self.h = num_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads
        self.qkv = nn.Linear(d_model, 3 * d_model, bias=False)
        self.proj = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.drop = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        B, T, C = x.shape
        # Single fused projection for Q, K, V
        q, k, v = self.qkv(x).split(C, dim=2)

        def reshape(t):
            return t.view(B, T, self.h, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)

        q, k, v = reshape(q), reshape(k), reshape(v)

        # Scaled dot-product with causal mask
        scale = 1.0 / math.sqrt(self.d_k)
        scores = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) * scale
        mask = causal_mask(T, device=x.device)
        scores = scores.masked_fill(mask[None, None], float("-inf"))
        weights = self.drop(scores.softmax(dim=-1))
        out = weights @ v  # (B, h, T, d_k)

        out = out.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, C)
        return self.proj(out)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    model = CausalMultiHeadAttention(d_model=256, num_heads=4)
    x = torch.randn(1, 20, 256)
    y = model(x)
    print("Output:", y.shape)  # (1, 20, 256)
Example 1 — Minimal Scaled Dot-Product Attention (NumPy, no dependencies)
This example demonstrates the core math without any deep learning framework, making it ideal for understanding the mechanics before encountering framework abstractions:

import numpy as np

def softmax(x, axis=-1):
    e = np.exp(x - x.max(axis=axis, keepdims=True))
    return e / e.sum(axis=axis, keepdims=True)

def scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, mask=None):
    """
    Q: (seq_len, d_k)
    K: (seq_len, d_k)
    V: (seq_len, d_v)
    Returns: output (seq_len, d_v), weights (seq_len, seq_len)
    """
    d_k = Q.shape[-1]
    scores = Q @ K.T / np.sqrt(d_k)   # (seq_len, seq_len)
    if mask is not None:
        scores = np.where(mask, -1e9, scores)
    weights = softmax(scores, axis=-1)
    output = weights @ V               # (seq_len, d_v)
    return output, weights

# Example: 4-token sequence, d_k=8, d_v=8
np.random.seed(42)
T, d_k, d_v = 4, 8, 8
Q = np.random.randn(T, d_k)
K = np.random.randn(T, d_k)
V = np.random.randn(T, d_v)

# Causal mask: upper triangle (i attends only to j <= i)
mask = np.triu(np.ones((T, T), dtype=bool), k=1)

out, attn = scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, mask=mask)
print("Output shape:", out.shape)   # (4, 8)
print("Attention weights (should be lower-triangular):")
print(np.round(attn, 3))
# Each row is a probability distribution over past+current tokens
Example 2 — Multi-Head Attention via PyTorch nn.MultiheadAttention
PyTorch ships a built-in MHA module. This example shows the calling convention, which differs slightly from the custom implementation above (it expects (T, B, d_model) unless batch_first=True):

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# PyTorch built-in MHA — use batch_first=True for (B, T, C) convention
mha = nn.MultiheadAttention(
    embed_dim=512,
    num_heads=8,
    dropout=0.0,
    batch_first=True,   # enables (B, T, C) input shape
    bias=False,
)

B, T = 2, 16
x = torch.randn(B, T, 512)

# Self-attention: Q = K = V = x
out, weights = mha(x, x, x)
print("Output:", out.shape)       # (2, 16, 512)
print("Weights:", weights.shape)  # (2, 16, 16) — averaged across heads

# Cross-attention: decoder queries from x, encoder keys/values from enc
enc = torch.randn(B, 32, 512)    # encoder output, 32 source tokens
out_cross, _ = mha(query=x, key=enc, value=enc)
print("Cross-attn output:", out_cross.shape)  # (2, 16, 512)

# Causal self-attention via attn_mask
causal_mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T), diagonal=1).bool()
out_causal, _ = mha(x, x, x, attn_mask=causal_mask)
print("Causal output:", out_causal.shape)  # (2, 16, 512)

In production encoder-decoder models (T5, BART, encoder-based summarization systems), a significant optimization is to pre-compute the key and value projections for the encoder output once per input sequence, then reuse them for every decoder step during autoregressive generation. This KV-cache brings cross-attention inference cost from O(T_dec * T_enc) per step to O(T_dec * d_k) — the encoder KV pairs are computed only once regardless of how many decoder tokens are generated.

At scale, this matters enormously: generating a 200-token summary from a 2000-token document with 12 decoder layers would otherwise recompute 24 matrix multiplications of shape (200, 2000) on every single token step. With KV caching, those 24 matrices are computed once.

"""
Cross-attention for an encoder-decoder Transformer (e.g. seq2seq translation).
The decoder queries attend to encoder key/value representations.
Demonstrates KV-cache for efficient autoregressive inference.
"""
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import math
from typing import Optional, Tuple

class CrossAttentionWithKVCache(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, num_heads: int):
        super().__init__()
        self.h = num_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads
        self.d_model = d_model
        self.W_Q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_K = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_V = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_O = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self._cached_k: Optional[torch.Tensor] = None
        self._cached_v: Optional[torch.Tensor] = None

    def encode(self, encoder_output: torch.Tensor):
        """Pre-compute and cache K, V from encoder output once per sequence."""
        B, S, _ = encoder_output.shape
        K = self.W_K(encoder_output).view(B, S, self.h, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        V = self.W_V(encoder_output).view(B, S, self.h, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        self._cached_k = K
        self._cached_v = V

    def forward(
        self,
        decoder_query: torch.Tensor,        # (B, T_dec, d_model)
        encoder_output: Optional[torch.Tensor] = None,
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        B, T, _ = decoder_query.shape

        if encoder_output is not None:
            self.encode(encoder_output)

        K, V = self._cached_k, self._cached_v
        Q = self.W_Q(decoder_query).view(B, T, self.h, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)

        scale = 1.0 / math.sqrt(self.d_k)
        scores = (Q @ K.transpose(-2, -1)) * scale
        weights = scores.softmax(dim=-1)
        out = (weights @ V).transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, self.d_model)
        return self.W_O(out)


# Usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    d_model, heads = 512, 8
    ca = CrossAttentionWithKVCache(d_model, heads)

    enc_out = torch.randn(4, 64, d_model)   # 4-batch, 64 src tokens
    ca.encode(enc_out)                       # compute KV once

    # Decode token-by-token; KV never recomputed
    for step in range(1, 11):
        dec_q = torch.randn(4, step, d_model)
        out = ca(dec_q)
        print(f"Step {step:2d}: output shape {tuple(out.shape)}")

This is the most common error when implementing MHA from scratch. Without the scaling, dot products grow large with d_k, softmax saturates to near-one-hot, and gradients vanish. Training will appear to proceed but the model will converge to poor solutions or diverge.

# WRONG: manually splitting with slicing — breaks gradient flow for W_Q/W_K/W_V
class BadMHA(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, num_heads):
        super().__init__()
        self.heads = num_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads
        # Only one weight matrix total, split at runtime — this is fine structurally
        # but the mistake is forgetting to scale before softmax:
        self.W_Q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.W_K = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.W_V = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        Q = self.W_Q(x).view(B, T, self.heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        K = self.W_K(x).view(B, T, self.heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        V = self.W_V(x).view(B, T, self.heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        # BUG: missing / math.sqrt(self.d_k) — softmax receives huge values
        # when d_k is large, pushing distribution to near-one-hot, vanishing gradients
        scores = Q @ K.transpose(-2, -1)
        weights = scores.softmax(dim=-1)
        return (weights @ V).transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, C)

# CORRECT: always scale before softmax
import math

class GoodMHA(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, num_heads):
        super().__init__()
        self.heads = num_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads
        self.W_Q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_K = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_V = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_O = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)

    def forward(self, Q_in, K_in, V_in, mask=None):
        B, T, C = Q_in.shape
        Q = self.W_Q(Q_in).view(B, T, self.heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        K = self.W_K(K_in).view(B, -1, self.heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        V = self.W_V(V_in).view(B, -1, self.heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        scale = 1.0 / math.sqrt(self.d_k)  # CORRECT: scale here
        scores = (Q @ K.transpose(-2, -1)) * scale
        if mask is not None:
            scores = scores.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        weights = scores.softmax(dim=-1)
        out = (weights @ V).transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, C)
        return self.W_O(out)
After transposing, the tensor is non-contiguous in memory. Calling
on a non-contiguous tensor raises a RuntimeError. Always call
before reshaping back from (B, h, T, d_k) to (B, T, d_model).

# WRONG — raises RuntimeError: view size is not compatible
out = attended.transpose(1, 2).view(B, T, d_model)

# CORRECT
out = attended.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, d_model)
# Alternative: use reshape() which handles non-contiguous tensors automatically
out = attended.transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, d_model)
Mistake 3 — Applying padding mask with wrong dtype or shape

When masking padded tokens, the mask must be broadcastable to (B, h, T, T) and must be a boolean tensor (for masked_fill) not float. A float mask multiplied before softmax is wrong; only additive -inf masking before softmax correctly zeros out pad positions.

# WRONG: float mask, wrong axis
pad_mask = (lengths.unsqueeze(1) > torch.arange(T))  # (B, T) float
scores = scores * pad_mask  # this does NOT properly mask

# CORRECT: boolean additive mask broadcast to (B, 1, 1, T_src)
# Positions where mask is True are masked OUT (replaced with -inf)
pad_mask = ~(lengths.unsqueeze(1) > torch.arange(T, device=lengths.device))  # (B, T)
pad_mask = pad_mask[:, None, None, :]  # broadcast over heads and query positions
scores = scores.masked_fill(pad_mask, float("-inf"))

Replace three separate Linear(d_model, d_model) calls with one Linear(d_model, 3*d_model) and split the output. This reduces memory bandwidth by roughly 3x and improves utilization on modern GPU tensor cores.

On PyTorch 2.0+, this function dispatches to Flash Attention or memory-efficient attention kernels automatically, yielding 3-5x speedups and significantly reduced memory usage compared to a naive implementation.
Biases in Q, K, V projections do not improve model quality in practice (the layer norm before MHA handles centering) and add unnecessary parameters.
Dropout on the weights matrix (after softmax) is the correct placement per the original paper, and prevents the model from relying on any single attention pattern.
Always assert divisibility. A model silently built with d_model=512 and h=6 would use d_k=85.3, which will truncate to 85 and cause subtle shape mismatches or wrong capacity.

Visualizing attention weights can quickly reveal pathological patterns: an attention head that only attends to the [CLS] token, uniform attention (a head has learned nothing), or attention always on the diagonal (a degenerate identity head).

For encoder-decoder models, call the encoder once and cache K and V projections for all decoder cross-attention layers before beginning generation. This is the single largest inference optimization available.
The computational complexity of a single MHA layer is dominated by two operations: the four linear projections (Q, K, V, O) at
. For short sequences (T < d_model), the projections dominate; for long sequences (T > d_model), the quadratic attention dominates. The crossover occurs at T ≈ d_model.

For GPT-3 (d_model=12288, h=96), at sequence length T=2048, each MHA layer requires approximately 2 * 96 * 2048^2 ≈ 800M FLOPs for attention alone, and 4 * 2048 * 12288^2 ≈ 1.2T FLOPs for the linear projections — the projections dominate at this sequence length.

Flash Attention (Dao et al., 2022) restructures the attention computation to operate in SRAM (L1/L2 cache) tile by tile, avoiding materializing the full T x T attention matrix in HBM. This reduces memory from O(T^2) to O(T) for the attention weights while maintaining exact numerical output. Flash Attention 2 further improves parallelism across the query dimension, and Flash Attention 3 adds async pipelining for H100 Tensor Cores.

Multi-Query Attention (MQA) and Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) are common production optimizations: they use a single set (or g sets) of K, V projections shared across all h query heads. LLaMA 2 (70B) and Mistral use GQA with g=8 query heads per KV head. This reduces KV cache memory by h/g — critical for serving large models where KV cache size limits batch capacity.
— reference implementation, supports both batch_first and legacy (T, B, C) conventions.
— dispatches to Flash Attention on CUDA, memory-efficient attention on CPU.
— context manager to force a specific attention kernel for benchmarking or debugging.
Most model classes expose an attention implementation through the
enables Flash Attention 2 for any supported model:

from transformers import AutoModel

# Flash Attention 2 requires flash-attn package: pip install flash-attn
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained(
    "bert-base-uncased",
    attn_implementation="flash_attention_2",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,  # Flash Attention requires fp16 or bf16
)

pip install flash-attn --no-build-isolation
# Requires CUDA 11.6+ and sm_80+ GPU (A100, H100, RTX 30xx+)
# After install, torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention
# automatically uses flash_attention_2 when available
Meta's xFormers library provides memory-efficient attention implementations including triton-based kernels, sparse attention patterns, and block-sparse variants. Install with
for an API-compatible drop-in with better memory behavior for longer sequences.
Long sequences with structured state; recurrent-style inference

MHA at long context; same math, faster kernel

State space models (SSMs) like Mamba have attracted significant research attention as potential competitors to MHA for long-sequence tasks. However, as of mid-2025, the dominant production architecture for large language models remains multi-head attention or its grouped-query variant (GQA). Hybrid architectures that interleave SSM layers with MHA layers show promising results on tasks requiring both local pattern recognition and global reasoning.
The softmax in attention is susceptible to overflow when scores are large. The standard mitigation is the "safe softmax" trick: subtract the row maximum before exponentiating. PyTorch's

does this automatically. If you implement softmax manually, always subtract the max. Flash Attention's online softmax algorithm extends this to the tiled computation, maintaining numerical equivalence to full-sequence softmax.

Attention sinks are a known phenomenon: certain tokens (often the first token or special tokens like [CLS]) accumulate disproportionately high attention weights across many heads, acting as "garbage collectors" for attention probability mass that has nowhere more informative to go. This is generally harmless but can mask pathological patterns. The StreamingLLM paper (Xiao et al., 2023) exploits attention sinks deliberately to enable infinite-length inference.

In agentic systems, adversarial inputs can exploit attention patterns. A retrieved document containing high-frequency "attention-stealing" tokens — tokens that consistently receive high attention due to learned statistical regularities — can shift the model's focus away from legitimate context. Mitigation strategies include explicit instruction hierarchies, attention supervision via fine-tuning, and output validation rather than relying on attention mechanisms for security boundaries.

Attention computation in float16 or bfloat16 can produce different results than float32 due to accumulation errors in the matrix multiplications. Flash Attention performs accumulation in float32 internally even when inputs are float16. When debugging numerical issues, switching to float32 is often the first diagnostic step. For deterministic inference, set
and use the same batch size — some attention kernels choose different code paths based on shape that can produce non-identical (though mathematically equivalent) results.
in h parallel subspaces and concatenates the results — the scaling by
is not optional; omitting it causes softmax saturation and vanishing gradients.

The "multi-head" aspect lets the model jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces simultaneously. Each head specializes on different patterns through training, without any explicit supervision on what to attend to.

In practice, all h heads are computed as a single batched matrix multiply by reshaping (B, T, d_model) to (B, h, T, d_k). There are never h separate weight matrices — just one large matrix that is logically split.
Naive MHA has O(T^2) memory cost. Flash Attention eliminates the full T x T matrix materialization via IO-aware tiling, enabling sequences of 100k+ tokens. In PyTorch 2.0+, use

The same MHA primitive serves three distinct roles in Transformers: encoder self-attention (bidirectional), decoder causal self-attention (lower-triangular mask), and encoder-decoder cross-attention (decoder queries over encoder KV pairs).

KV caching is the most important inference optimization for encoder-decoder models: compute K and V projections for the encoder once and reuse them for every decoder step. Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) reduces KV cache size in decoder-only models by sharing K/V heads across groups of query heads.

Multi-Head Attention is permutation-equivariant — it has no built-in positional awareness. Positional encodings (sinusoidal, learned, rotary, ALiBi) are an external addition and must be considered as a separate design choice orthogonal to MHA itself.

Attention weights are interpretable in principle but should not be treated as ground-truth explanations. High attention weight means the model "looked at" that position but does not guarantee the information was causally important to the output.
